prompt:
Use image 1 and image 2 only as pose-and-structure references. Use image 3 only as the final official design-language reference for face, hair, eye color, robe design, trim, and academy mood. Use image 4 only as the approved pose-lock result that must preserve body-facing direction, lean, and idle silhouette. This pass is a design-finish pass built on top of the stage-1 pose lock.

Keep only these source facts from the original image and preserve them exactly: exact body-facing direction, exact body lean, exact torso tilt, exact head angle, exact eye direction, exact shoulder line, exact hand placement, exact pelvis position, exact leg spacing, exact knee height, exact calf width, exact foot placement, and exact overall body placement inside the frame. Preserve the full visible body from hair to shoes. Keep the exact composition, empty padding, and center alignment of the original source frame. Do not redesign the anatomy, the pose, or the character placement inside the frame.

Never mirror the pose. Never flip the facing direction. Never turn the original right-facing idle into a left-facing or front-facing pose. Do not make the stance symmetrical. Do not make the shoulders square to the camera. Keep the same rightward lean and right-facing idle read as both the original source keyframe and the approved stage-1 pose-lock sample.

This is a neutral idle sprite keyframe. Both hands must remain empty. Do not show fire, magic flame, spell orb, book, card, scroll, wand, or any active combat effect. Do not convert the idle into a casting pose. Keep the original idle hand pose exactly.

Replace the entire source outfit with a full academy robe outfit. Preserve only body pose and structural placement. Do not keep any source coat, scarf, shoulder cloth, cape tail, belt buckle, sash, chest badge, tunic panel, short outerwear layer, boot design, hanging front ornament, hood lining color, or source collar color from the source image. A full-length robe silhouette must replace the source outerwear silhouette.

Design a handsome teenage academy mage for Dungeon Mage. He is a trained elite student mage taking his graduation exam in the labyrinth. He should read as kind, intelligent, disciplined, sharp, and cool, but not villainous. For the in-game sprite proportion, rebuild him as a compact 3.5-head SD character. Keep the original gameplay pose and frame placement, but change the visual proportion read so the head, torso, and legs feel like a polished 3.5-head action RPG sprite rather than a taller illustration. He should still read as lean and athletic, not bulky, with a slimmer torso read, lighter cheek volume, a cleaner jawline, and a slightly longer leg read inside that compact 3.5-head body design.

Face: show a youthful elite mage face with flatter cheeks, a narrower lower face, and a cleaner sharper jawline. Give him a defined nose bridge, restrained mouth shape, and long narrow angular eyes with a stronger upper-eyelid line and a cooler more focused expression than a gentle fantasy schoolboy. The brow line should look focused and intelligent. The expression should combine calm self-possession, sharp concentration, and a faint mischievous confidence.

Eyes: iris color must be clearly ruby red at first glance, not black, not brown, not gray, and not neutral dark.

Hair: overwrite the source hair color completely. The hair must read clearly as bright blond at first glance, not brown. Use bright golden blond hair with a slight orange warmth inside the blond range. Use a neat aristocratic academy-student hairstyle with about half of the hair brushed or swept back.

Outfit overview: this is a robe, not a cape, not a coat, and not a tunic. The robe is the main outfit and must dominate the silhouette. The robe color should be a near-black very dark blue, clearly blue-black and still visibly blue in the shading, never plain black, red, gray, purple, magenta, or violet. The robe and visible lining should stay in the same blue-black family, never purple. Use a small but readable amount of metal ornament: restrained gold trim at selected robe edges, a small academy crest detail, and subtle gold accents at the cuffs.

Robe structure: show a true full robe that hangs straight downward with weight like a heavy academy gown. The outer silhouette should fall vertically beside the body. The robe wraps the front, back, sides, shoulders, and arms of the body instead of flying behind the character. The robe must not flare backward, trail like a cape, behave like a scarf, or flutter in idle. Show no backward cloth wing shape and no loose scarf tail. The left and right front robe panels hang vertically from the shoulders and meet near the center front. The robe front must read as mostly closed cloth panels extending from the shoulders down toward mid-shin, not as an open coat. Only one narrow vertical chest opening remains at the center front, about seventy to eighty percent of the face width. Through that opening, show a clearly readable but still limited glimpse of a dark vest in the same blue-black family as the robe, a white shirt, and a clearly red tie.

Robe fit and length: do not add any belt, waist sash, waist strap, waist ornament, belt buckle, hanging cord, horizontal waist band, or waist-cinching accessory. The robe falls cleanly from shoulders and torso. The waist should not have a separate strap, band, seam, or buckle read. The robe length reaches about mid-shin. The robe covers most of the front of the body and does not open wide like a coat. Use only a narrow controlled center slit below the waist so the pelvis, legs, trousers, and shoes remain readable without turning the robe into a coat silhouette.

Lower body: trousers should be straight slacks in the same dark blue-black family as the robe. Shoes should be simple low dress shoes, not boots, in a dark formal leather style that matches the robe palette. They must read as low shoes at the ankle, with no boot shaft, no calf coverage, and no armored footwear read.

Rendering style for in-game asset: high-resolution dot graphic character sprite, polished 2D game sprite, online action RPG style, Korean fantasy RPG aesthetic, pixel-based rendering with refined detail, smooth silhouette, clean anatomy simplification, rich costume layering, readable costume design, expressive anime-inspired face, crisp outlines, controlled cel shading, subtle pixel texture, vibrant fantasy palette, premium in-game character asset, transparent background. This must not look like low-resolution retro pixel art, not blocky, and not minimalist. It should feel like a premium modern side-view battle sprite prepared as a high-resolution production source. Build forms with discrete tone groups and visible pixel-like clusters rather than painterly gradients. Use about four to seven tone groups per major material region. Use selective one-pixel anti-aliasing only on chosen silhouette and face edges. Use shadow and highlight clusters that read like two to six pixel groups at 4x upscale. Avoid long soft airbrushed gradients, oil-paint blending, and smooth illustration rendering. Generate this pass as a real 1024x1024 high-resolution working image that remains compatible with later 128x128 runtime reduction.

Important: preserve the original keyframe composition exactly, preserve the original right-facing idle body direction, keep both hands empty, keep the approved stage-1 pose-lock body read, and do not redesign the anatomy or pose.
